title: Trauma and the Body's Truth: Honoring What Your Body Knows
excerpt: Explore how trauma lives in the body and why honoring bodily signals is crucial for healing and nervous system regulation.
body:
Introduction: Beyond Talk Therapy—Listening to the Body

If you’ve been in therapy but still feel stuck, you’re not alone. Trauma isn’t just a story stored in the mind—it’s embedded in the body. The body holds a truth that words often can’t reach. As Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score, makes clear, trauma rewires your nervous system and lodges deep in your physical experience.

This article explores the science behind how trauma imprints on the body, why honoring these somatic signals matters, and how somatic healing methods can help regulate the nervous system and free you from stuck patterns.

What Trauma Does to the Body

Trauma is not just an event; it’s the body’s response to overwhelming stress that exceeds its ability to cope. Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, explains that traumatic experiences get stored as tension and dysregulation in the nervous system, often outside conscious awareness. This can manifest as chronic pain, tension, anxiety, or emotional numbness.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory offers a framework for understanding these responses. The autonomic nervous system (ANS) shifts between states of safety, mobilization (fight/flight), and immobilization (freeze/shutdown). When trauma hijacks this system, the body gets stuck in defensive modes, leading to symptoms like hypervigilance, dissociation, or shutdown.

The Body Keeps the Score

Van der Kolk’s extensive research shows that trauma survivors often have altered brain-body communication. For example, trauma affects the insula and anterior cingulate cortex—areas responsible for body awareness—leading to a disconnection from bodily sensations. This disconnection complicates traditional talk therapy, making somatic approaches invaluable.

Why the Body’s Truth Matters

Ignoring or suppressing the body’s signals only prolongs suffering. The body knows what the mind can’t always articulate. Deb Dana, a leading clinician specializing in Polyvagal-informed therapy, stresses the importance of listening to the body’s cues as reliable data about safety and threat.

“The body is the original storyteller of trauma. Healing begins when we listen deeply to what it’s been trying to say all along.” – The Oracle Lover

By honoring sensations like tightness, fluttering, or heaviness, you reclaim agency over your nervous system. This awareness fosters safety, which is the foundation for rewiring trauma responses.

Somatic Healing: Techniques That Work

Somatic healing isn’t about reliving trauma verbally. It’s about creating a safe internal environment where the nervous system can renegotiate and discharge trapped energy.

  • Somatic Experiencing (SE): Developed by Peter Levine, SE uses mindful awareness of bodily sensations to track and release traumatic energy gently.
  • Trauma-Sensitive Yoga: Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy combines movement and mindfulness to restore body-mind connection.
  • Polyvagal-Informed Practices: Deb Dana’s interventions focus on tuning into vagal states, moving out of fight/flight or freeze, and cultivating safety.
  • Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR): Francine Shapiro’s technique uses bilateral stimulation to reprocess traumatic memories stored somatically.

Each method respects the body’s pace, avoiding retraumatization while fostering regulation.

Nervous System Regulation as the Healing Core

Dan Siegel highlights the importance of integration—linking the body, mind, and brain for balanced regulation. Trauma disrupts this integration, fragmenting experience and increasing dysregulation.

Self-regulation practices such as breath work, grounding, and mindful movement help restore these connections. Neuroscience shows that repeated activation of the parasympathetic nervous system (the calming branch) through these practices builds resilience.

Internal Family Systems (IFS) and Somatic Awareness

Richard Schwartz’s IFS model identifies different internal parts or subpersonalities that often hold trauma. Somatic awareness is key for dialogue with these parts, helping to unburden stuck emotions and sensations. This bridges mind and body in a practical way.

The Role of Compassion and Patience

Gabor Maté emphasizes that trauma healing requires a compassionate stance toward oneself. The body’s responses are survival strategies, not flaws. Recognizing this reduces shame and fosters a nurturing inner environment.

Healing is nonlinear and often slow. The body’s truth unfolds gradually, requiring patience and consistent presence.

Practical Steps to Honor Your Body’s Truth

  • Notice Sensations: Begin by simply observing bodily feelings without judgment.
  • Create Safety: Use grounding techniques like feeling your feet on the floor or slow breathing.
  • Move Gently: Engage in slow, mindful movement such as stretching or yoga.
  • Seek Somatic Therapy: Find practitioners trained in SE, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or Polyvagal approaches.
  • Practice Self-Compassion: Remind yourself that your body’s reactions are normal and protective.

Conclusion: Trusting the Body to Guide Healing

The latest neuroscience and trauma research—from van der Kolk to Porges, Levine to Dana—point to one truth: the body is the key to trauma recovery. It’s time to listen.

faqs.q: Why does trauma get stored in the body?
faqs.a: Trauma overwhelms the nervous system’s capacity to process stress, causing it to get trapped as physical tension, dysregulation, and altered nervous system states outside conscious awareness.

faqs.q: How does somatic healing differ from traditional talk therapy?
faqs.a: Somatic healing focuses on bodily sensations and nervous system regulation rather than verbal processing alone, addressing trauma stored in the body that words can’t fully reach.

faqs.q: What is the Polyvagal Theory and why is it important?
faqs.a: Developed by Stephen Porges, Polyvagal Theory explains how the autonomic nervous system shifts between states of safety, fight/flight, and freeze, shaping trauma responses and healing potential.

faqs.q: Can self-directed somatic practices help with trauma healing?
faqs.a: Yes, practices like mindful breathing, grounding, and gentle movement can support nervous system regulation and increase body awareness, but working with trained somatic therapists enhances safety and effectiveness.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that explains how trauma reshapes the body and brain, offering insights into somatic healing.

metaTitle: Trauma and the Body's Truth: Honoring What Your Body Knows
metaDescription: Discover why trauma lives in the body and how somatic healing helps regulate your nervous system for lasting recovery.
author: The Oracle Lover
